title: Code: The Hidden Language of Computer Hardware and Software
author: Charles Petzold
subtitle: Understanding the Inner Workings of Computers from First Principles
description: A remarkable journey that starts with the simplest possible communication system — a flashlight and Morse code — and builds up, step by step, to the complete architecture of a modern computer. Petzold explains how binary codes, logic gates, memory, and processors work together, showing that computers are not magical but are built from layers of simple, understandable components.
keyIdeas: All computer communication is based on codes — from Morse to binary
Boolean algebra is the mathematical foundation of digital logic
Logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) are the building blocks of all digital circuits
Flip-flops enable memory — storing a single bit of information
Adders perform arithmetic using nothing but logic gates
The CPU is a machine that follows a stored program of instructions
Memory (RAM) is organized as an addressable array of bits
Operating systems manage resources and provide abstractions over hardware
Machine language is the native tongue of the CPU that everything compiles to
Protocols enable communication between different layers and systems
keyTakeaways: Computers are not magic — they are built from incredibly simple components layered together
All digital information is encoded as binary — 1s and 0s — and everything flows from that
Boolean algebra (AND, OR, NOT) maps directly to electronic circuits called logic gates
Memory is built from flip-flops that can store one bit each, organized into addressable arrays
Arithmetic (addition, subtraction, multiplication) is performed by networks of logic gates
The CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions stored in memory — this is the stored-program concept
Machine language is the lowest level of programming; everything above is abstraction
Operating systems provide essential abstractions — processes, files, memory management
Protocols define how computers communicate at every level, from hardware to the web
Understanding how computers work at every level makes you a better programmer
whoShouldRead: Programmers who want to understand what happens below the code they write
Computer science students seeking a clear, intuitive understanding of hardware
Curious non-programmers who want to understand how computers actually work
Self-taught developers who missed the fundamentals of computer architecture
